publications.title: Structural basis for clonal diversity of the human T-cell response to a dominant influenza virus epitope.
publications.authors: Yang X, Chen G, Weng NP, Mariuzza RA.
publications.executive_summary: HLA-A2-restricted CD8+ T cells recognize the immunodominant influenza A virus epitope GILGFVFTL (GIL) through TCRs that fall into two classes: canonical TCRs using TRAV27/TRBV19 (∼60% of the response) and non-canonical TCRs with diverse gene usage (∼40%). This study determined the 1.7 Å crystal structure of a non-canonical TCR (F50, TRAV13-1/TRBV27) bound to GIL-HLA-A2 and compared it to a canonical TCR (JM22) previously crystallized on the same ligand, revealing that F50 and JM22 adopt markedly different binding orientations—crossing angles of 29° versus 69°, different focal points along the MHC α1 helix, and distinct residues (tryptophan versus arginine) engaging the GIL-HLA-A2 interface. This structural diversity explains how a single, featureless peptide epitope can be recognized through multiple independent molecular solutions, enabling a polyclonal TCR repertoire that protects against viral escape mutations and maintains protection even if individual TCR clones are lost. The findings underscore how clonal diversity in anti-viral immunity derives not from a single optimal TCR-pMHC interaction but from multiple structurally distinct yet functionally competent solutions to the same recognition problem.
